coredata.dc:title: Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle decorated at single-wall carbon nanotubes: An excellent, powerful electrocatalyst for the fabrication of An electrochemical sensor to determine nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol as two opioid analgesic drugs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Tavana, Toktam
Rezvani, Ali Reza
Karimi-Maleh, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle decorated at the surface of single-wall carbon nanotubes (Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs) was synthesized using a simple chemical precipitation method and characterized by XRD, TEM, and EDS methods. The results confirmed that Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs were synthesized with good purity and at the nanoscale size. Moreover, a highly sensitive electroanalytical sensor was fabricated by incorporating synthesized Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposites into a carbon paste electrode (CPE) in the presence of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium methanesulfonate (EMICH3SO3 −) as binder. The Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE showed a powerful electro-catalytic activity for electro-oxidation of nalbuphine, and the results confirmed that the oxidation of nalbuphine was improved 6.34 times and relative oxidation potential was decreased about 110 mV compared to unmodified electrodes. The Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE also showed good catalytic activity for the determination of nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol and the oxidation potential of these opioid analgesic drugs separated with ΔE =460 mV. In the final step, the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE was used to determine nalbuphine with a detection limit of 0.9 nM and tramadol with a detection limit of 50.0 nM in drug samples. The results confirmed the powerful and interesting ability of the sensor in the analysis of a real sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nalbuphine
Tramadol
1-Ethyl-3-methylimidazolium methanesulfonate
Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle-decorated single-wall carbon nanotubes

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE as the sensor
Electrochemical behavior of nalbuphine at surface of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE
Simultaneous determination of nalbuphine and tramadol
Interference study and real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of nalbuphine (a) and tramadol (b).
A) TEM and B) XRD pattern of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs nanocomposite.

The E-pH carve relative to electro-oxidation of 500 μM nalbuphine at surface of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE. Inset) cyclic voltammograms of 500 μM nalbuphine at surface of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE at pH range 5.0 - 9.0.
Cyclic voltammograms of 500.0 μM nalbuphine at surface of a) CPE, b) Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/CPE, c) EMICH3SO3 −/CPE and d) Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE recorded at scan rate 100 mV/s.
The cyclic voltammograms of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE in the solution containing 350 μM nalbuphine at scan rate a) 25, b) 50, c) 80, d) 120 and e) 200 mV/s.
Tafel plot for Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in the presence of 350 μM nalbuphine.

A) Chronoamperograms obtained at the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE in the solution containing (a) 1.0 mM, (b) 2.0 mM and (c) 3.0 mM nalbuphine. B) Cottrell plots obtained from chronoamperometry.

A) The SW voltammograms of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE in the presence solution containing a) 25.0 + 30.0; b) 45.0 + 45.0; c) 70.0 + 80.0; d) 105.0 + 110.0 and e) 140.0 + 150.0 μM nalbuphine + tramadol. B) The plots of the Ipa as a function of nalbuphine concentration. C) The plots of the Ipa as a function of tramadol concentration.
The analytical parameters for analysis of nalbuphine using electrochemical sensors.
Determination of nalbuphine and tramadol in real samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle decorated at single-wall carbon nanotubes: An excellent, powerful electrocatalyst for the fabrication of An electrochemical sensor to determine nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol as two opioid analgesic drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Sistan and Baluchestan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, University of Sistan and Baluchestan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanostructure Based Biosensors Research Group, Ton Duc Thang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNanostructure Based Biosensors Research Group, Ton Duc Thang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Applied Sciences, Ton Duc Thang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFaculty of Applied Sciences, Ton Duc Thang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle decorated at the surface of single-wall carbon nanotubes (Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs) was synthesized using a simple chemical precipitation method and characterized by XRD, TEM, and EDS methods. The results confirmed that Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs were synthesized with good purity and at the nanoscale size. Moreover, a highly sensitive electroanalytical sensor was fabricated by incorporating synthesized Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposites into a carbon paste electrode (CPE) in the presence of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium methanesulfonate (EMICH3SO3 −) as binder. The Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE showed a powerful electro-catalytic activity for electro-oxidation of nalbuphine, and the results confirmed that the oxidation of nalbuphine was improved 6.34 times and relative oxidation potential was decreased about 110 mV compared to unmodified electrodes. The Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE also showed good catalytic activity for the determination of nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol and the oxidation potential of these opioid analgesic drugs separated with ΔE =460 mV. In the final step, the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE was used to determine nalbuphine with a detection limit of 0.9 nM and tramadol with a detection limit of 50.0 nM in drug samples. The results confirmed the powerful and interesting ability of the sensor in the analysis of a real sample.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Pt-Pd-doped NiO nanoparticle-decorated single-wall carbon nanotubes

ce:section.ce:para: Opioid analgesics comprise a wide range of drugs suggested for the management of pain, [1,2] including buprenorphine, tramadol, fentanyl, methadone, dextromethorphan, oxycodone, meperidine, nalbuphine, and codeine, each of which has different side effects and has been suggested for the treatment of pain for many years [3]. Nalbuphine (Scheme 1a), or nubain, is one of the important opioid analgesics used to treat pain that is prescribed in different forms, including injection [4,5]. Like other opioid analgesic compounds, nalbuphine has been shown to have many side effects, including dizziness, sweatiness, dry mouth, nausea, sedation, vomiting, vertigo, clamminess, and headache [6]. Compared to some other opioid analgesics, like morphine, the administration of nalbuphine is less risky [6]. Tramadol (scheme 1 b) is another opioid analgesic used for the management of pain [7]. Scientific results have shown that the combination of nalbuphine and tramadol can control postoperative pain in children [8]. Nevertheless, high doses of opioid analgesics in the body can create major problems for human health [9,10]. Analytical methods such as HPLC, spectroscopy, and electrochemical techniques are influential tools for determining drug concentrations in biological and pharmaceutical samples [11–19]. Among the analytical methods used for drug analysis, electrochemical techniques have shown more advantages for this goal because of their fast response and portability [20–29]. Electrochemical techniques have more diversity than other analytical sensors in that they are better able to be modified [30–40]. Using nanomaterials, ionic liquids, DNA, enzyme, aptamer, and other relative conductive materials gives electrochemical sensors the ability to be selective and usable in sensitive analysis procedures [41–51]. Meanwhile, due to interesting structural changes, nanomaterials have individual features and have been proposed for various applications [52–57]. In addition, ionic liquids with conductive structure is other powerful mediators useful mediators for fabrication electroanalytical sensors in recent years [58–62].

Because of the above-mentioned benefits, the current study aimed to design and fabricate a novel, highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for determining nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol, two important opioid analgesic drugs. For this goal, a Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposite as a conductive catalyst was synthesized and then used to fabricate a Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE as an electroanalytical sensor for determining nalbuphine.

All chemical materials such as nalbuphine hydrochloride hydrate (98 %), phosphoric acid, SWCNTs−COOH (> 90 % carbon basis), tramadol hydrochloride, graphite powder, and 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium methanesulfonate (≥ 95 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate, platinum (II) acetylacetonate (50 % Pt), and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Merck Company. Palladium (II) chloride, 59 % Pd was purchased from ACROS Company. The nalbuphine and tramadol stock solutions (0.01 M) were prepared by dissolving 0.0412 g and 0.0263 g of analytes in 100 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS) water of specific conductance (0.3 – 0.8 μS).

All relative electrochemical investigations were performed on an Ivium-Vertex, Potentiostat/Galvanostat machine with IviumSoft software. The Potentiostat/Galvanostat device was connected to an electrochemical cell with Ag/AgCl/KClsat, (as reference electrode), Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE (as working electrode), and Pt wire (as counter electrode). The TEM machine model Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV Company (Germany) was used to investigate TEM. The crystalline structural of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs nanocomposite was characterized by an XRD instrument of model X’Pert Pro.

0.5 M nickel nitrate hexahydrate + 0.5 g functionalized SWCNTs with carboxylic acid +8 mg platinum (II) acetylacetonate and 8.0 mg palladium (II) chloride were dispersed in 100 mL water solution and stirred for 1.5 h under ultrasonication. Then, 100 mL sodium hydroxide (1.0 M) was added during stirring for 1.5 h. The black precipitated samples were filtered, dried for 1.0 h at 80 °C, and calcinated at 600 °C for 4.0 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE as the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE was prepared using slurry composed of 0.06 g Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs + 0.94 g graphite powder and using 13 drops of paraffin oil and 2 drops of EMICH3SO3 − as binders using a mortar and pestle. The mixture was hand mixed for 4 h and a homogeneous paste was achieved. The paste was placed in a glass tube and attached to an electrical contact (copper wire).

Nalbuphine injection (20 mg/mL) and tramadol injection (50 mg/mL) were purchased from a local pharmacy, and then directly used as real samples to check the performance ability of the sensor in determining nalbuphine and tramadol using the standard addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of nalbuphine at surface of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE
ce:section.ce:para: According to a report by Fouladgar and because of phenolic structure of nalbuphine [33], it is suggested that the redox behavior of this drug is relative to the pH of the solution. In pH-dependence compounds, the analytical data strongly depends on the alteration of the pH solution, and the optimization of this factor is very important in the fabrication of a sensitive sensor. Therefore, in the first step, focus was put on the optimization of this factor in the pH range of 5.0–9.0. As can be seen in the Fig. 3 inset, the oxidation signal of nalbuphine improved when the pH value was increased from pH = 5.0 to pH = 7.0, and then it decreased. Simultaneously, the oxidation potential of nalbuphine shifted to a negative value with increasing pH, and the results showed a linear relation against pH with the equation EP = 0.063 pH + 0.971 (R2 = 0.997). This confirmed that equal values of electrons and protons are present in the redox mechanism of this opioid analgesic drug that suggested two electron and two proton in this case. According to the recorded signals, the maximum current for electro-oxidation of nalbuphine can be observed at pH = 7.0, and that was selected for the next step in the current investigation. In the next step, the modification process and the catalytic effects of mediators at the surface of a CPE in the presence of nalbuphine were investigated (Fig. 4 ). Cyclic voltammograms of 500.0 μM nalbuphine were recorded at the surface of a CPE (curve a), Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/CPE (curve b), EMICH3SO3 −/CPE (curve c), and Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE (curve d). The results showed that the oxidation current of nalbuphine at the surface of the CPE was very weak and had a current 15.3 μA with an oxidation potential of 530 mV. After modification of the CPE with Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposite and with EMICH3SO3 −, the oxidation signal of nalbuphine improved to 61.2 μA and 68 μA, respectively. In addition, the oxidation potential of nalbuphine decreased from 680 mV at the CPE to 640 mV and 630 mV at the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/CPE and EMICH3SO3 −/CPE, respectively. This data confirmed that the modification of the CPE with Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposite or EMICH3SO3 − can create a highly sensitive electroanalytical sensor for the determination of nalbuphine. Based on the above points, the CPE was modified with Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposite and EMICH3SO3 −, and the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE showed a synergic effect for the oxidation behavior of nalbuphine. The oxidation signal of nalbuphine increased to 97.0 μA, and the oxidation potential of the drug decreased to 530 mV after modification of a CPE with Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs and EMICH3SO3 −. The active surface area of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE, EMICH3SO3 −/CPE, Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/CPE and CPE were calculated using the standard method ([Fe(CN6)3-,4-] (1.0 mM) in the presence of KCl (0.1 M), and the results showed an active surface area 0.339 cm2, 0.315 cm2, 0.305 cm2 and 0.253 cm2 for the four electrodes, respectively. Using the obtained currents (Fig. 4) and the active surface area data, the current density results relative to nalbuphine oxidation at the surface of unmodified and modified electrodes was drawn in the Fig. 4 inset. Based on the results, it was concluded that mediators can improve conductivity as well as the active surface area of sensors.

The current study also investigated the type of movement of the species to the electrode surface. To do so, the cyclic voltammogram of 350.0 μM nalbuphine was recorded at the surface of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE in the scan rate range of 25–200 mV/s. The linear relationships between the oxidation current of nalbuphine and ν 1/2 with equation I p = 8.6494 ν 1/2 – 11.3690 (R2 = 0.9946) were observed, and the results suggested a diffusion process for the electro-oxidation of nalbuphine at the surface of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE (Fig. 5 ). In addition, the positive shift in oxidation potential of nalbuphine at the surface of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE with an increase in scan rate showed an irreversible process for the redox behavior of nalbuphine (Fig. 5 inset).

According to the results in Fig. 5, the diffusion process controlled the oxidation behavior of nalbuphine; thus, attempts were made to determine the diffusion coefficient (D) of nalbuphine using the chronoamperometric method. The chronoamperograms of 1.0 mM, 2.0 mM, and 3.0 mM nalbuphine at the surface of the Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE using the applied potential of 650 mV (Fig. 7 A). The relative Cottrell plots were drawn, and the results are presented in Fig. 7B. As observed with the obtained slopes and Cottrell equation [I = nFAD1/2C / (□1/2 t1/2)], the mean value of D was calculated to be 5.06 × 10-5 cm2/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of nalbuphine and tramadol
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the importance of the analysis of nalbuphine in the presence of tramadol in biological and pharmaceutical samples, the ability of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE to determine nalbuphine was investigated. First, the square wave voltammograms of nalbuphine and tramadol were recorded separately. The results showed that Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE can detect nalbuphine in the concentration range of 0.004–850 μM with the equation I = 0.3531 Cnalbuphine + 3.3733 (R2 = 0.9973) and tramadol in the concentration range of 0.1–750.0 μM with the equation I = 1.1276 Ctramadol + 4.7321 (R2 = 0.9976). The limits of detection were 0.9 nM and 50.0 nM for nalbuphine and tramadol, respectively. The analytical results such as LOD and LDR for nalbuphine is better than previous electrochemical suggested sensors (Table 1 ) [33,63,64]. Next, the square wave voltammograms of nalbuphine and tramadol in a solution containing different concentrations of each were recorded (Fig. 8 A). The results showed two separated oxidation signals with the potentials of about 540 mV (relative to nalbuphine) and 1000 mV (relative to tramadol). These separate potentials were sufficient for the simultaneous determination of nalbuphine in the solution containing tramadol. The current-concentration curves relative to this investigation are shown in Fig. 8 B and C, and their slopes were compared with those in the linear dynamic range. As can be seen, the sensitivity of these plots is very similar with that of the slopes obtained in the linear dynamic range, which confirmed that the presence of tramadol did not interfere in the determination of nalbuphine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In analytical sensors, selectivity is a key factor in the design and fabrication of a new sensor. Therefore, the selectivity of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE for determining 20.0 μM nalbuphine under optimum conditions was checked. The results showed that in the solution containing 20.0 μM nalbuphine, 400-fold amounts of glucose, buprenorphine, methadone, ascorbic acid (after addition of ascorbic oxidase), codeine, oxycodone, and alanine and 1000-fold amounts of K+, Mg2+, Na+ and Cl− showed no interference in the determination of nalbuphine.

The ability of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE to determine nalbuphine and tramadol in injection samples was checked using the standard addition method. The recovery data between 99.18–101.6 % for nalbuphine and 102.2–102.26 % for tramadol confirmed the powerful ability of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE as an electroanalytical sensor for real sample analysis (see Table 2 ).

The stability of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE was checked in the time period of 45 days. Square wave voltammograms of 20.0 μM nalbuphine were recorded at different time periods, and the results were investigated. The results indicated that 92 % of the initial oxidation current of nalbuphine remained after 45 days and a 5% error in the oxidation potential of nalbuphine was also observed. These results confirmed the good stability of Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE as an electroanalytical sensor.

In this research, a synthetic nanocomposite with amazing electrical properties (Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs) was described. The synthesized Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNT nanocomposite was characterized, and the results confirmed the synthesis of a pure and nano-size nanocomposite. The synthesized nanocomposite was used as a conductive and excellent electrocatalyst for the modification of carbon paste electrodes. Two electrodes amplified with Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs and EMICH3SO3 − showed an amazing catalytic activity and the improved redox behavior of nalbuphine. Moreover, Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE showed a powerful ability to determine nalbuphine with a detection limit of 0.9 nM in the presence of tramadol. In the final step, Pt-Pd/NiO-NPs/SWCNTs/EMICH3SO3 −/CPE was tested for the determination of nalbuphine and tramadol in a real sample with the recovery data of 99.18–101.6 % for nalbuphine and 102.2–102.26 % for tramadol, respectively.

Toktam Tavana: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ali Reza Rezvani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration. Hassan Karimi-Maleh: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is not any conflict interest in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Placebo and opioid analgesia--imaging a shared neuronal network

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Petrovic, E. Kalso, K.M. Petersson, M. Ingvar, Placebo and opioid analgesia--imaging a shared neuronal network, Science, 295 (2002) 1737-1740.

Cobb, Length of stay and opioid dose requirement with transversus abdominis plane block vs epidural analgesia for ventral hernia repair, J. Am. Coll. Surg. 228 (2019) 680-686.

F. Mathai, F. Portugal, Z. Mehta, M. Davis, Nalbuphine# 381, J. Palliat. Med. 22 (2019) 1471-1472.

L.J. Scott, C.M. Perry, Tramadol, Drugs, 60 (2000) 139-176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure-based discovery of opioid analgesics with reduced side effects

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validation of HPLC multi-residue method for determination of fluoroquinolones, tetracycline, sulphonamides and chloramphenicol residues in bovine milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Moudgil, J.S. Bedi, R.S. Aulakh, J.P.S. Gill, A. Kumar, Validation of HPLC multi-residue method for determination of fluoroquinolones, tetracycline, sulphonamides and chloramphenicol residues in bovine milk, Food Anal. Methods, 12 (2019) 338-346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of phenolic acids and flavonoids in Artemisiae Argyi Folium by HPLC-MS/MS and discovery of antioxidant ingredients based on relevance analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-X. Xia, B.-B. Zhao, J.-F. Zan, P. Wang, L.-L. Chen, Simultaneous determination of phenolic acids and flavonoids in Artemisiae Argyi Folium by HPLC-MS/MS and discovery of antioxidant ingredients based on relevance analysis, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal. 173 (2019) 120-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sulfamethoxazole and trimethoprim in pharmaceuticals by visible and UV spectrophotometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Shamsa, L. Amani, Determination of sulfamethoxazole and trimethoprim in pharmaceuticals by visible and UV spectrophotometry, Iran J. Pharm. Res. 5 (2006) 31-36.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of glucose in dried serum samples by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Petibois, V. Rigalleau, A.-M. Melin, A. Perromat, G. Cazorla, H. Gin, G. Déléris, Determination of glucose in dried serum samples by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, Clin. Chem. 45 (1999) 1530-1535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensors, a bright future in the fabrication of portable kits in analytical systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Karimi‐Maleh, F. Karimi, M. Alizadeh, A.L. Sanati, Electrochemical Sensors, a Bright Future in the Fabrication of Portable Kits in Analytical Systems, Chem. Rec. 20 (2020) https://doi.org/10.1002/tcr.201900092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of the monodisperse Pt/Ni@ rGO nanocomposite synthesized by ultrasonic hydroxide assisted reduction method in electrochemical nonenzymatic glucose detection
